transcript.text: Design a flash-sale / limited-inventory system: sell exactly K items with no overselling under a massive concurrent spike (e.g. 1M users for 1K items). Focus on the inventory decrement race at scale, admission control / queueing, and fairness.

blocks.md:
# Design a Flash Sale System

> The problem: sell **exactly K items** (e.g. 1,000 sneakers) to a crowd of **1,000,000 concurrent buyers** the instant the sale opens — with **zero overselling**, fair access, and a site that doesn't fall over. The whole game is the inventory-decrement race at extreme concurrency, plus admission control so the spike never reaches the parts that can't take it.

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — the buy path, the waiting room, the reservation lifecycle. After that I'll go deep on the three hardest parts: preventing oversell on the hot inventory counter, admission control, and the reservation-timeout race — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: **1M people want the same 1,000 units in the same second.** Inventory is a single shared number that everyone is trying to decrement at once. If two buyers both read "1 left" and both decrement, we oversold — and overselling a physical item means refunds, angry customers, and lost trust. So the design is really *one hard problem* (a contended counter) wrapped in machinery to keep the stampede away from it.

> 🏢 I'll target an **Amazon SDE-3 bar**: cost and operations are first-class, I work backwards from the customer (a fair shot at the item, a clear "you got it / you didn't"), and I'll decide and move rather than enumerate every option.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — Browse the sale & see live availability.** Users should be able to open the product page before and during the sale and see whether stock remains.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Anyone can pull up the sneaker page and see 'available' or 'sold out', even if that number is a second or two behind reality."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** 1M people refreshing one page is 500K reads/s on a *single* object — the read storm alone can melt an origin if it isn't fully cached at the edge.

**FR2 — Join the sale & be admitted fairly (virtual waiting room).** When demand exceeds what the buy path can serve, users should be placed in a waiting room and admitted in a controlled, fair order.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If too many people show up at once, we put them in a digital line and let them through a few at a time, in roughly the order they arrived — like a bouncer at a club door."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** you must throttle 3M buy-attempts/s down to a trickle the inventory tier can absorb, *without* letting anyone slip the queue, and the queue itself can't become the new bottleneck.

**FR3 — Attempt to buy (atomic reserve, no oversell).** An admitted user should be able to claim one unit; the system either grants a hold or tells them it's sold out — and never grants more than K holds total.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When it's your turn, you tap Buy and instantly find out 'it's yours, pay now' or 'sorry, gone' — and we can never hand out more 'it's yours' than we actually have."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is the binding bottleneck — a single counter being decremented by a torrent of concurrent writers, where a single missed lock = a real oversell.

**FR4 — Check out & pay within a time limit (reservation → order).** A buyer who holds a reservation should complete payment within a short window; if they don't, the unit is released back to the pool.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Once you've got a hold, you have two minutes to pay. Don't pay in time and the shoe goes back on the shelf for someone else."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the hold must be released *exactly once* on timeout, payment must be idempotent (no double-charge on retry), and the released unit must safely re-enter the count without reintroducing an oversell.

**Below the line (out of scope):** product catalog management & merchandising, recommendations/search, post-sale shipping & logistics, returns/refunds workflow, fraud/bot detection beyond basic rate limiting (I'll mention it but not design it), and multi-item carts (flash sale is one SKU, quantity one).

### Non-Functional (talkable targets)

**NFR1 — No overselling, ever (correctness over everything).** Hard invariant: granted holds + completed orders ≤ K, always.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We will never sell the 1,001st unit of a 1,000-unit drop, full stop."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** two buyers pay for the last pair, we cancel one order, eat a refund, and a customer who 'won' now feels cheated — the single worst outcome for this product.

**NFR2 — Survive the spike without falling over (availability ~99.9% during sale, graceful shed above that).** The buy path should answer in **p99 < 200 ms** for admitted requests; the read/page path stays up at 500K reads/s.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Three nines means under ~9 hours of downtime a year, but more importantly: the site doesn't crash when a million people hit it at once — extra load gets a polite waiting-room page, not an error."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the whole spike hits the database, connection pools exhaust in seconds, and *everyone* sees a 500 — including the people who would have bought.

**NFR3 — Fairness (bounded, best-effort FIFO admission).** Admission should be roughly first-come-first-served; no one with a faster bot should consistently beat patient humans.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If you got in line first, you should generally get your shot first — we don't want bots vacuuming the whole drop."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the entire inventory goes to scalpers/bots in milliseconds, real customers never get a chance, and the brand takes the reputational hit.

**NFR4 — Idempotent, exactly-once money.** A given checkout must charge at most once even under client retries, network timeouts, and crashes.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If your phone retries the payment because the network blipped, you still only get charged once."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** retries during the chaos double-charge customers — a trust and compliance disaster.

> 💬 "The whole design lives or dies on one shared number — the inventory count — so I'm going to spend most of my budget protecting that counter and keeping the crowd away from it, not on the boring parts."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So functionally there are four flows: see the item and whether it's available, get admitted through a fair waiting room, atomically claim a unit without overselling, and pay within a timeout or lose the hold. The non-functionals that actually shape the design are: never oversell — that's a hard invariant; stay up under a million concurrent users by shedding load gracefully instead of crashing; be fair so bots don't eat everything; and make money idempotent so retries don't double-charge. Everything hard about this problem is the single contended inventory counter, so that's where I'll go deep."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "Is the inventory truly scarce and fixed at K, or is it effectively unlimited like a digital coupon?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** if stock is unlimited I can throw away the entire reservation-and-decrement machinery; if it's a hard K, that race *is* the problem.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If scarce (K=1,000) → atomic decrement + reservations + waiting room are the core. If unlimited → just rate-limit and log; no counter contention at all.
- **Assumption:** scarce, fixed K (1,000), physical goods. This is the hard, interesting case.

**Q2 — "Roughly how many concurrent users hit at the open — thousands, or a million?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** the spike size decides whether one Redis key survives or whether I *must* add admission control and possibly shard the counter.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If ~50K → a single atomic counter handles it, skip the waiting room. If ~1M → admission control is mandatory (we'll see the math forces it).
- **Assumption:** ~1M concurrent at T0 (the brief). This forces a virtual waiting room.

**Q3 — "Do we own the payment processor, or is it an external gateway like Stripe with its own latency and failure modes?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** an external processor takes hundreds of ms and can fail — so I must keep payment *outside* any lock and make it async, otherwise the lock-hold time kills throughput.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If external → reserve-first, pay-second saga with timeout release. If in-house → I could in theory do a tighter transaction, but I'd still split it for resilience.
- **Assumption:** external processor (~800 ms p99, can fail). Reserve-then-pay saga.

**Q4 — "How strict is fairness — strict global FIFO, or is rough first-come-first-served plus anti-bot good enough?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** strict global ordering across a million clients is extremely expensive (a global sequencer); rough FIFO per shard is cheap and usually what the business actually wants.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If strict → a global token issuer / sequencer (a coordinator, with its own ceiling). If rough → per-region queues with timestamped tokens, far cheaper.
- **Assumption:** rough best-effort FIFO + basic per-account/IP rate limiting. Throughput and no-oversell beat perfect ordering.

**Q5 — "Is one unit per customer the rule, and how long do they get to pay?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** the per-user limit changes dedup keys, and the payment window sets the reservation TTL and how fast unpaid stock recycles.
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If 1-per-user → dedup on user_id at reserve time. If N-per-user → quantity logic and a different decrement.
- **Assumption:** 1 unit per customer; 2-minute payment window (reservation TTL = 120s).

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to assume a hard 1,000-unit drop, ~1M concurrent users, an external payment processor, rough FIFO fairness, and one-per-customer with a two-minute pay window. If any of those is off — especially the scale or the fairness strictness — tell me now, because they change the design materially."

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

All arithmetic below was computed by running `capacity.py`; I'm surfacing only the rounded, say-out-loud figures.

> **Glossary, glossed once:** **QPS** = queries (requests) per second. **Atomic decrement** = a single indivisible "subtract one and tell me the result" that no other writer can interleave with. **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a value lives before it auto-expires.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Oversubscription | 1,000,000 users ÷ 1,000 items | 1000:1 | "a thousand buyers chasing every single unit" | Why fairness + admission matter at all |
| Peak buy QPS (raw) | 1M users x ~3 retries ÷ 1s spike | ~3.0M/s | "about three million buy taps in the first second" | The number that forces admission control |
| Single hot-key ceiling | one Redis node, one key ~ 100K ops/s | ~100K/s | "one Redis key tops out near a hundred thousand a second" | The binding bottleneck ceiling |
| Overload factor | 3.0M ÷ 100K | ~30x | "we're thirty times over what one counter can take" | MUST shed load before the counter |
| Admitted buy QPS | throttle the spike to what the tier serves | ~10K/s | "we drip about ten thousand real attempts a second through" | Sizing the admission rate (300x cut) |
| Time to sell out | 1,000 successful decrements ÷ 10K/s | ~100 ms | "the 1,000 units are gone in a tenth of a second" | Waiting room is mostly showing 'sold out' |
| Status-poll reads | 1M users ÷ 2s poll interval | ~500K/s | "half a million status checks a second" | Read tier must be edge-cached |
| Origin reads after cache | 500K x (1 − 99.9% hit) | ~500/s | "after the CDN, the origin sees only ~500/s" | One cache layer makes reads a non-issue |
| Buy-path p99 budget | 15+20+10+5+25+25 ms (sum) | ~100 ms | "an admitted buy clears in about a tenth of a second" | Keeps us under the 200 ms SLO |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** **~3M buy-attempts/s vs a ~100K/s single-key ceiling = 30x over.** A single Redis counter physically cannot absorb the spike.
**Flip threshold:** if peak *admitted* demand stays under **~80K/s**, one Redis key is enough and I'd skip the waiting room entirely. Above that, admission control is mandatory. (Counter *sharding* only helps when **K itself is large** — for K=1,000 the issue is write rate, not the count, so admission is the right lever, not sharding.)

*Storage is a non-issue here* — 1,000 reservations and at most 1,000 orders is a few KB; this problem is about write contention and load shedding, not bytes.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything: about three million buy taps land in the first second, but a single Redis counter tops out near a hundred thousand a second — so we're thirty times over what one key can take. That's why I can't just point a million people at a counter; I have to shed load first with a waiting room, dripping maybe ten thousand real attempts a second through, at which point the thousand units sell out in about a tenth of a second. Reads are easy by comparison — half a million status checks a second, but a CDN absorbs 99.9% so the origin sees only a few hundred. So my whole budget goes on protecting that one counter, not on storage or reads."

---

## 4. Core Entities

> This is a first draft of the nouns; I'll add full field definitions in §7.

**InventoryCounter** — the single source of truth for "units remaining" for a sale. Owns the atomic decrement that decides winners.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a turnstile with a hard cap — it lets exactly K people through and then physically locks."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do two buyers racing for the last unit not both win?" → the decrement is atomic and returns the post-value; only the writer who sees a result ≥ 0 wins, everyone else gets sold-out.

**Reservation** — a temporary hold on one unit for one user, created at the moment of a successful decrement, with a TTL.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a 'hold' tag on the shelf — set aside for you for two minutes, then it goes back if you don't pay."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "What if the user never pays?" → on TTL expiry a release worker returns the unit to the counter — exactly once.

**Order** — the durable record created when payment succeeds; the reservation's terminal, paid state.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "the receipt — proof the sale actually happened and money changed hands."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can one reservation become two orders on retry?" → no; the idempotency key on checkout collapses retries to one order.

**WaitingRoomToken (AdmissionToken)** — a signed pass that says "you're allowed to attempt a buy now," issued by the admission controller in fair order.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a numbered ticket from the deli counter — when your number's called you can step up, not before."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can someone fake a token to skip the line?" → no; tokens are signed and short-lived, validated server-side at the buy endpoint.

**Sale** — the campaign config: which SKU, K, start time, per-user limit, reservation TTL.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "the event poster — what's on sale, how many, when the doors open."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you arm the sale at exactly T0?" → the counter is pre-loaded with K and the gate flips at start_time; before that, all buys are rejected at the edge.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The nouns are: the InventoryCounter — the one shared number everything fights over and the heart of the design; the Reservation, which is a short-lived hold created the instant you win a decrement; the Order, the durable paid record; the AdmissionToken, the signed 'it's your turn' pass from the waiting room; and the Sale config. The one to watch is the InventoryCounter — it's the single coordinator, and almost every hard question traces back to it. I'll flesh out the fields in the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

**Security stance (say this once, applies everywhere):** the caller's identity comes from their **JWT** *(a signed login token the server can verify without a database lookup)* — never from a `userId` in the body. The client never sends price or quantity-it-wants-to-pay; the server reads K, price, and limits from the Sale config. The client cannot forge state (`status`, `reservationId`) — the server sets those.

### FR1 — Read availability → `GET /v1/sales/{saleId}`
- **Why GET:** pure read, fully cacheable at the edge.
- **Key fields:** `saleId` (path). No body.
- **Example:**
```
GET /v1/sales/drop_air_2026
// Response 200 (served from CDN, ~1s stale)
{ "saleId": "drop_air_2026", "status": "LIVE", "remaining": "available", "startsAt": "2026-06-21T17:00:00Z" }
```
- **Key decision:** the response says `"available"` / `"sold_out"`, **not an exact count** — exposing the precise number invites scraping and makes the cache churn on every decrement. Coarse status is cacheable for ~1s.

### FR2 — Join the waiting room → `POST /v1/sales/{saleId}/queue`
- **Why POST:** creates server-side state (your place in line).
- **Key fields:** `saleId` (path); identity from JWT. No client-set position.
- **Example:**
```
POST /v1/sales/drop_air_2026/queue
// Response 202
{ "queueToken": "qt_8f...", "position": 48213, "etaSeconds": 35, "pollAfterMs": 2000 }
```
- **Key decision:** returns `202 Accepted` with a `pollAfterMs` — the client polls a status endpoint; when admitted it receives a short-lived signed `admissionToken`. Position is **server-assigned** from arrival time (rough FIFO), never client-supplied.

### FR3 — Attempt to buy / reserve → `POST /v1/sales/{saleId}/reservations`
- **Why POST:** creates a Reservation (new state).
- **Key fields:** `admissionToken` (proves it's your turn — server-validated, signed); `Idempotency-Key` header (so a retry doesn't double-reserve). Identity from JWT. **No price, no quantity** — server enforces 1-per-user from config.
- **Example:**
```
POST /v1/sales/drop_air_2026/reservations
Headers: Idempotency-Key: 2c1a-...   Authorization: Bearer <jwt>
{ "admissionToken": "at_d4..." }
// Response 201 (you won)
{ "reservationId": "rsv_77a", "status": "HELD", "expiresAt": "2026-06-21T17:02:00Z", "amount": 18900, "currency": "usd" }
// Response 409 (sold out)
{ "error": "SOLD_OUT" }
```
- **Key decision:** the **atomic decrement happens here** and is the only place inventory changes. The `amount` is set by the **server** from the Sale config — the client never names a price. Same `Idempotency-Key` returns the *same* reservation, not a second one.

### FR4 — Pay & confirm → `POST /v1/reservations/{reservationId}/checkout`
- **Why POST:** transitions the reservation to a paid Order.
- **Key fields:** `paymentMethodId` (a token from the processor — we never see the raw card, keeping us out of **PCI scope** *(the rules for handling card data)*); `Idempotency-Key` header.
- **Example:**
```
POST /v1/reservations/rsv_77a/checkout
Headers: Idempotency-Key: 9b3e-...
{ "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123" }
// Response 200
{ "orderId": "ord_55c", "status": "CONFIRMED" }
// Response 410 (hold expired before you paid)
{ "error": "RESERVATION_EXPIRED" }
```
- **Key decision:** payment runs **outside any lock** (the unit is already reserved). The idempotency key makes a retried checkout return the existing order instead of charging twice. If the hold expired, you get `410` and the unit is already back in the pool.

> 💬 "Notice the client never sends a price or a userId or its own queue position — the server owns all of that. The only things the client supplies are signed tokens and an idempotency key."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four endpoints, one per flow. A GET on the sale returns a coarse available-or-sold-out — coarse on purpose, so it caches and doesn't leak the exact count. POSTing to the queue gets you a place in line and, when it's your turn, a signed admission token. POSTing to reservations is where the one atomic decrement lives — that's the whole ballgame — and it returns HELD or SOLD_OUT. Then checkout pays outside any lock with an idempotency key so retries don't double-charge. Throughout, identity is from the JWT and price is from our DB, never the request — the client can't tamper with who they are or what they pay."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)

> **Color palette (used in every diagram):** blue = clients, green = our services, amber = queues/async, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 "Browse the sale & see live availability"

Framing: a million people stare at one page and poll its status — this is a read-fan-out problem solved almost entirely at the edge.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN edge cache | A global network of caches near users that serves the page without touching our servers | One hot object polled 500K/s — the edge absorbs 99.9% so origin sees ~500/s | Not origin-only (500K/s melts it); not per-request DB read (pointless for one object) | Status is ~1s stale (fine — it's coarse anyway) |
| Read service | A small stateless service that renders the coarse status when the edge misses | Owns the available/sold-out logic and TTL | Not reading the live counter per request (that re-creates contention) | Slightly stale status |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a fleet of caches spread worldwide that serve content from near the user. Here it absorbs the 500K/s page-and-status storm so our origin barely notices.
> **Origin** — our actual servers behind the CDN. Here it only sees cache misses, ~500/s.

**Diagram — read path (FR1)**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Buyer app]:::client
    U -->|"1 GET sale status"| CDN[CDN edge cache]:::ext
    CDN -->|"2 miss only"| RS[Read service]:::svc
    RS -->|"3 read coarse status"| KV[(Status cache Redis)]:::store
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2a5c,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: none (first slice).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Buyer requests sale status.** Hits the nearest CDN edge. *Why here:* the edge is the only thing that can take 500K/s. *State:* none changed. *What if:* edge node down → CDN routes to another edge automatically.
2. **On a miss, edge calls the read service.** *Why:* the read service owns the coarse status and TTL. *State:* none. *What if:* read service overloaded → it's stateless, autoscale; worst case serve last cached value.
3. **Read service reads coarse status from Redis.** *Why:* a tiny in-memory read, never the contended counter. *State:* none. *What if:* Redis blip → serve `LIVE` optimistically; the buy path is the real gate, so a stale "available" just sends a few people to a 409.

> 💬 "The trick is we never read the *exact* count on the hot path — we serve a coarse available-or-sold-out that caches for a second, so a million pollers turn into a few hundred origin reads."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "For browsing, a million people polling one page is half a million reads a second — way too much for an origin. So I put it all behind a CDN, which is just a global cache near users; it absorbs about 99.9% and the origin sees a few hundred a second. And I deliberately show a coarse available-or-sold-out instead of the exact count, so the cache stays warm and we don't leak the live number to scrapers."

### 6.2 "Join the sale & be admitted fairly (virtual waiting room)"

Framing: the spike is 30x what the counter can take, so before anyone reaches inventory we put them in a line and admit a controlled rate in arrival order.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Admission controller | A service that hands out a place in line and, at a fixed rate, signed "your turn" tokens | Converts a 3M/s stampede into a steady ~10K/s admitted trickle — a 300x cut | Not letting everyone hit inventory (30x over ceiling); not a single global lock (that IS the bottleneck) | Some users wait; ordering is rough not perfect |
| Queue store | A Redis sorted-set keyed by arrival time, holding positions | Cheap rough-FIFO ordering, in-memory, fast | Not a durable queue like Kafka per-user (overkill — losing a waiting-room slot is recoverable) | Queue is not durable; on crash, re-queue |
| Rate gate | A token bucket that releases admissions at the target rate | Lets us tune admitted QPS to exactly what inventory can absorb | Not unthrottled (re-creates the spike) | Admitted rate is a tuned dial, set per sale |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Virtual waiting room** — a digital line that holds users and admits them gradually. Here it sheds the spike so inventory never sees more than it can handle.
> **Token bucket** — a rate limiter that releases a fixed number of permits per second. Here it sets the admitted buy rate (~10K/s).
> **Sorted set** — a Redis structure that keeps members ordered by a score. Here the score is arrival time, giving rough FIFO.
> **Admission token** — a short-lived signed pass proving it's your turn. Here it's the only way to reach the buy endpoint.

**Diagram — admission path (FR2)**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Buyer app]:::client
    U -->|"1 join queue"| AC[Admission controller]:::svc
    AC -->|"2 add by arrival time"| QS[(Queue store sorted set)]:::store
    AC -->|"3 release at rate"| RG[Rate gate token bucket]:::svc
    RG -->|"4 mint admission token"| U
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: Buyer app (from §6.1).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **User joins the queue.** Admission controller assigns a position by arrival timestamp. *Why:* one place owns fair ordering. *State:* a queue entry created. *What if:* duplicate joins → dedup on user_id, one slot per user.
2. **Entry added to the sorted set by arrival time.** *Why:* O(log n) ordered inserts, in-memory fast. *State:* queue grows. *What if:* Redis blip → re-queue on reconnect; losing a slot just re-times you.
3. **Rate gate releases admissions at the tuned rate.** *Why:* a token bucket caps admitted QPS to what inventory can absorb. *State:* a batch of users marked admitted. *What if:* gate set too high → inventory tier sees pressure; we tune it below the safe ceiling.
4. **Admitted user is minted a signed admission token.** *Why:* the buy endpoint trusts only this token. *State:* user can now attempt a buy. *What if:* token forged → signature check fails at the buy endpoint.

> 💬 "Admission control is the whole reason this works — we turn a three-million-a-second stampede into a ten-thousand-a-second trickle, a 300x cut, before anyone touches the counter."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Because the spike is thirty times what one counter can take, I put a virtual waiting room in front of inventory — basically a bouncer. Everyone joins a Redis sorted set ordered by arrival time, and a token-bucket rate gate releases people at a tuned rate, say ten thousand a second, handing each a signed admission token. That's a 300x reduction, and the buy endpoint only trusts that token, so nobody can skip the line. Fairness is rough, not perfect, but that's the right trade — throughput and no-oversell matter more than strict ordering."

### 6.3 "Attempt to buy (atomic reserve, no oversell)"

Framing: this is the binding bottleneck — the one place inventory changes — and the entire design exists to feed it a safe, throttled stream.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reservation service | Validates the admission token, runs the atomic decrement, writes the hold | Owns the single mutation point for inventory | Not letting clients touch Redis directly (no auth/validation) | Extra hop (~25 ms, within budget) |
| Inventory counter (Redis) | A single in-memory integer decremented atomically by a Lua script | Atomic DECR-and-check is the cleanest no-oversell primitive at this speed | Not a Postgres row with SELECT FOR UPDATE (lock + disk = far slower under spike); not optimistic retries (thundering herd) | In-memory; needs durable mirror for crash recovery |
| Reservation store | Durable record of each hold with a TTL | Survives a Redis crash; source for the timeout-release worker | Not Redis-only (volatile — a crash would lose holds) | One extra write (~25 ms) |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Lua script (in Redis)** — a tiny program Redis runs atomically as one indivisible step. Here it decrements the counter and creates the hold so no other writer can interleave.
> **SELECT FOR UPDATE** — a SQL row lock held until the transaction commits. Considered and rejected here — it serializes on disk and is too slow for the spike.
> **Atomic compare-and-decrement** — "if count > 0, subtract one, else reject," done indivisibly. Here it's the no-oversell guarantee.

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Admitted user attempts to reserve, presenting the token.** Reservation service validates the signature. *Why:* the service is the only authenticated path to inventory. *State:* none yet. *What if:* token expired → 401, user re-queues.
2. **Service runs the atomic decrement (Lua).** *Why:* one indivisible "decrement if >0" makes the race impossible. *State:* counter drops by one *iff* it was positive. *What if:* counter already 0 → script returns sold-out, no write.
3. **Only a non-negative result wins.** *Why:* the decrement returns the post-value; ≥0 means you got a real unit. *State:* winner determined. *What if:* concurrent writers → Redis serializes them; exactly K can win.
4. **Service writes the hold with a TTL to the durable reservation store.** *Why:* survives a Redis crash and feeds the timeout worker. *State:* a HELD reservation exists. *What if:* write fails after decrement → compensating return-to-counter (see §8 failure matrix).
5. **Emit a `reserved` event.** *Why:* downstream (analytics, notifications) consume async. *State:* event appended. *What if:* event log down → buy still succeeds; events replay later.

> 💬 "Two buyers can't both win the last unit because the decrement is one atomic step — Redis serializes them, and only the writer who sees a result of zero or more actually got a shoe."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "This is the heart. The reservation service validates your admission token, then runs a tiny Lua script in Redis that says 'if count is above zero, subtract one and return the new value, else reject' — all in one indivisible step. That single atomicity is the entire no-oversell guarantee: Redis serializes every writer, so exactly a thousand of them can come back non-negative. I rejected a Postgres row lock because locking and disk under a three-million-a-second spike is far too slow. The trade is the counter is in memory, so I also write a durable hold and keep a mirror for crash recovery."

### 6.4 "Check out & pay within a time limit (reservation → order)"

Framing: payment is slow and external, so it runs outside the lock; an unpaid hold must release exactly once on timeout.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Checkout service | Charges via the processor using an idempotency key, then writes the Order | Money must be exactly-once and outside any inventory lock | Not charging inside the decrement (lock held across 800 ms = throughput death) | A reservation can expire before payment (acceptable, releases unit) |
| Payment processor | External gateway (e.g. Stripe) that charges the card | We don't store cards (PCI scope) | Not in-house payments (out of scope, huge build) | ~800 ms latency, can fail — handled by saga |
| Release worker | A scheduled worker that returns expired holds to the counter | Recycles unpaid stock so it isn't lost | Not relying only on Redis TTL (TTL frees the hold but must also re-increment the counter exactly once) | Slight delay (seconds) before stock recycles |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Idempotency key** — a client-supplied unique id so a retried request is recognized and not re-executed. Here it ensures one charge and one order even under retries.
> **Saga** — a sequence of steps with compensating actions instead of one big transaction. Here: reserve → pay → confirm, with "release on timeout" as the compensation.
> **Release worker** — a background job that returns expired holds to inventory exactly once. Here it recycles unpaid stock.

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Buyer submits checkout with an idempotency key.** *Why:* checkout owns the money step. *State:* none yet. *What if:* duplicate retry → same key returns the existing order, no second charge.
2. **Checkout charges the processor with the key.** *Why:* external gateway keeps us out of PCI scope. *State:* charge attempted. *What if:* processor times out → retry with same key; charge is idempotent on their side too.
3. **Charge succeeds.** *Why:* payment is the slow external step, kept outside any lock. *State:* money captured. *What if:* charge fails → reservation stays HELD until TTL, then released.
4. **Checkout writes the Order durably.** *Why:* the order is the terminal paid record. *State:* CONFIRMED order exists. *What if:* crash after charge before write → reconcile by idempotency key (§8).
5–6. **Release worker returns expired, unpaid holds to the counter exactly once.** *Why:* recycle stock without re-oversell. *State:* counter incremented; reservation marked EXPIRED. *What if:* worker runs twice → idempotent release (guarded by reservation status flip) prevents double-increment.

> 💬 "Payment is slow and external, so it never sits inside the lock — the unit is already reserved; we just have a two-minute clock, and if it runs out the release worker puts the shoe back exactly once."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Once you hold a unit, checkout charges the external processor with an idempotency key, so a retry returns your existing order instead of charging twice, then writes a durable confirmed order. Critically, payment runs outside any lock — the unit's already yours for two minutes. If you don't pay in time, a release worker returns the unit to the counter exactly once and the next person can grab it. It's a saga: reserve, pay, confirm, with timeout-release as the compensation."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — Entity table**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key) | Chosen store | Partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| InventoryCounter | **saleId (PK)**, remaining:int | Redis (in-memory) + durable mirror | saleId | Strong (atomic, single key) |
| Reservation | **reservationId (PK)**, saleId, userId, status, expiresAt | Postgres (durable) | reservationId | Strong |
| Order | **orderId (PK)**, reservationId, userId, amount, status | Postgres | orderId | Strong |
| AdmissionToken | token (signed, not stored), userId, exp | none (stateless signed) | — | n/a (verify by signature) |
| Queue entry | **userId (PK)**, saleId, arrivalTs:score | Redis sorted set | saleId | Best-effort (volatile) |
| Sale | **saleId (PK)**, sku, K, startAt, ttl, price | Postgres | saleId | Strong |

**PART B — Storage decision cards**

**🗄️ Redis — used for: InventoryCounter, Queue entries, coarse status cache**
1. **What it is:** an in-memory key-value store that executes single-key operations atomically and extremely fast.
2. **Why — the access pattern that forces it:** we need a *single-key atomic compare-and-decrement* at ~10K admitted writes/s with sub-millisecond latency. Only an in-memory store with atomic primitives (a Lua script) gives us a no-oversell decrement this fast.
3. **Considered & rejected:** *Postgres row + SELECT FOR UPDATE* — correct but locks on disk; under the spike the connection pool exhausts. *DynamoDB conditional update* — works for the count but adds cross-region latency and is harder to make a clean single-flight decrement at this rate. *Optimistic retries in app* — thundering-herd retries under contention.
4. **Trade-off:** in-memory = volatile; we need a durable mirror + reservation store so a crash doesn't lose the truth.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Inventory lives in Redis because I need an atomic decrement at ten thousand a second with sub-millisecond latency — a Lua script does 'subtract one if positive' indivisibly. I rejected a Postgres row lock because locking on disk under this spike kills the connection pool. The cost is Redis is in-memory, so I keep a durable mirror and a durable reservation record for recovery."

**🗄️ Postgres — used for: Reservation, Order, Sale**
1. **What it is:** a relational database with ACID transactions — a multi-step write either fully commits or fully rolls back.
2. **Why — the access pattern that forces it:** orders and reservations are money-adjacent and must be durable, queryable, and transactionally consistent; the reservation→order transition and idempotency dedup need real transactions.
3. **Considered & rejected:** *MongoDB* — fine schema-wise but we want battle-tested ACID for money; *Redis-only* — volatile, unacceptable for orders; *DynamoDB* — workable but we'd rebuild transactional guarantees the relational DB gives for free.
4. **Trade-off:** lower raw write throughput than Redis — fine, because only ≤1,000 reservations/orders ever exist per sale.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Reservations and orders go in Postgres because this is money — I want ACID durability and clean idempotency dedup. Volume is tiny, at most a thousand rows, so Postgres throughput is never the issue; correctness is."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency**

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decrement inventory | Redis | Strong (atomic) | Two racers can't both win — the invariant |
| Write reservation hold | Postgres | Strong | Durable, feeds timeout worker |
| Charge + write order | Postgres | Strong + idempotent | Money: exactly-once |
| Release expired hold | Redis + Postgres | Strong, idempotent | Re-increment exactly once |
| Read coarse status | CDN/Redis | Eventual (~1s) | Display tolerates staleness |
| Read-your-own-reservation | Postgres primary | Strong (read-from-primary after write) | The buyer must immediately see their own HELD reservation |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Two stores. Redis holds the inventory counter and the queue — it's in-memory, so I get a sub-millisecond atomic decrement, which is the no-oversell guarantee. Postgres holds reservations, orders, and the sale config, because that's money and I want ACID durability and clean idempotency. I considered a single Postgres row with a lock for inventory but rejected it — disk locking under this spike exhausts the connection pool. The read display is eventual, ~1 second stale, which is fine; but a buyer reads their own reservation from the primary so they see their hold immediately."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one invariant — granted holds ≤ K — and ask "what's the single thing that can violate it here?" The whole design defends that one number.

### How do we prevent overselling on the hot inventory counter?

**#### Bad: Read-modify-write in the app**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Read 'how many left', subtract one in code, write it back."
- **Approach:** app reads remaining, checks >0, writes remaining−1.
- **Why people try this:** it's the obvious, intuitive thing.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** it's a race — two of the ~10K admitted/s read "1 left" between each other's writes and *both* write 0, overselling. At any real concurrency this oversells immediately.
- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** any concurrency at all — even two simultaneous buyers — breaks it.

**#### Good: Atomic decrement in Redis (single key)**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** the read and write weren't one step, so writers interleaved. We make decrement-and-check a single indivisible operation.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Make 'subtract one if any are left' a single uninterruptible action the store does for us."
- **Approach:** a Lua script in Redis runs `if count>0 then count=count-1; return count else return -1` atomically. Redis serializes all writers on that key; only results ≥0 win. Exactly K winners, ever.
- **Why reasonable:** Redis single-key ops are atomic and fast (~100K/s).
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** Redis is in-memory — a node crash loses the counter; and one key tops out near ~100K ops/s. For K=1,000 with ~10K admitted/s that's fine, but if admitted demand exceeds ~80K/s the single key saturates.
- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** admitted demand above the single-key ceiling, **or** the durability gap (crash recovery).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Writer 1]:::client
    W2[Writer 2]:::client
    W1 -->|"decr if positive"| IC[(Inventory counter Redis)]:::store
    W2 -->|"decr if positive"| IC
    IC -->|"serialized one at a time"| L[Lua atomic step]:::svc
    L -->|"result ge zero wins"| W1
    L -->|"result lt zero sold out"| W2
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
```

**#### Great: Atomic decrement + admission throttle + durable mirror + sharded stripes for large K**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good was correct but fragile — it relied on the spike *not* exceeding the single-key ceiling, and it wasn't crash-safe.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Keep the atomic decrement, but never let more than a safe trickle reach it, mirror it to disk so a crash can't lose it, and if the item count itself is huge, split the counter into stripes."
- **Approach:** (1) Admission control caps writes at ~10K/s — comfortably under the ~100K/s ceiling. (2) Every decrement is also appended to a durable log / AOF (append-only file) so on crash we replay to the exact remaining. (3) For *large K* (e.g. 1M units), split the counter into N stripes each holding K/N; a writer hashes to a stripe and decrements there, with a thin reconciliation when a stripe hits zero (steal from a non-empty stripe). This removes the single-key write ceiling.
- **Why this is the answer:** correctness from atomicity, survivability from throttle + durability, and unbounded headroom from striping when K is large.
- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** raw spike 3M/s ÷ single-key ceiling 100K/s = **30x over** → admission is mandatory. With admission, 10K/s ≪ 100K/s ceiling → one key suffices for K=1,000. Striping only earns its keep when admitted write rate would exceed ~80K/s (i.e. very large K with very high admitted throughput).
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Two buyers race last unit | Redis serializes; one gets 0, one gets −1 (sold-out) |
| Redis node crashes | Replay AOF / promote replica to exact remaining; in-flight holds re-derived from Postgres reservations |
| Decrement succeeds, hold write fails | Compensating re-increment of counter; user sees retryable error |
| Release worker runs twice | Reservation status flip guards it — second run is a no-op |
| Stripe empties while others have stock | Reconciler rebalances from a non-empty stripe before declaring sold-out |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The no-oversell guarantee is one atomic Redis decrement — a Lua script that does 'subtract one if positive' indivisibly, so two racers can't both win. To make it survivable I do three things: admission control keeps writes at ten thousand a second, well under the hundred-thousand ceiling; I mirror every decrement to an append-only log so a crash replays to the exact count; and for genuinely huge inventories I'd shard the counter into stripes with a reconciler. For a thousand-unit drop, throttle plus one atomic key plus a durable mirror is exactly enough."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not Postgres SELECT FOR UPDATE?":** "It's correct but it locks a row on disk and holds it across the transaction — under a spike the fixed connection pool, maybe 200 connections, exhausts in seconds and the whole DB stalls. Redis's single-key atomic op is in-memory and serializes without holding a client-visible lock."

### How do we shed the spike fairly — admission control / waiting room?

**#### Bad: No throttle, everyone hits inventory**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Let all million people slam the buy endpoint."
- **Approach:** no queue; first writes win.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** 3M/s vs 100K/s ceiling = 30x over; the counter and the whole buy tier collapse; *nobody* buys.
- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** the 30x overload.

**#### Good: Token-bucket rate limit at the gateway**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** no throttle at all. Add a rate limiter that drops excess.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A guard that only lets ten thousand a second through and rejects the rest."
- **Approach:** a token-bucket limiter at the gateway admits ~10K/s and 429s the rest.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** it's *unfair* — it drops randomly, so a patient user is as likely to be rejected as a bot, and rejected users hammer retries, re-creating load.
- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** fairness (NFR3) and retry storms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Many buyers]:::client
    U -->|"buy attempts"| TB[Token bucket gate]:::svc
    TB -->|"under limit pass"| RV[Reservation service]:::svc
    TB -->|"over limit reject 429"| U
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
```

**#### Great: Virtual waiting room with ordered admission**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good dropped people randomly and invited retry storms. Replace random drop with an ordered queue that *holds* people and admits them, so they wait instead of retry.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A real numbered line — you join once, you keep your place, and you're let in when your number comes up, so you don't keep banging on the door."
- **Approach:** users join a Redis sorted set scored by arrival time. A rate gate admits the front of the line at ~10K/s, minting signed admission tokens. The client polls its position (cached, cheap) instead of retrying the buy. Tokens are short-lived so admitted-but-idle slots recycle.
- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** 3M/s spike → 10K/s admitted = **300x reduction**; at 10K/s, the 1,000 units sell out in ~100 ms, so the room mostly serves "sold out, you're position N" gracefully.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Bot floods joins | One slot per user (dedup) + per-IP rate limit on joins |
| Queue store crashes | Re-queue on reconnect; losing a slot just re-times you (recoverable) |
| Admitted user never buys | Token expires, slot effectively recycled; inventory unaffected |
| Sells out mid-queue | Remaining users get a clean sold-out, no buy attempt reaches inventory |

    QS -->|"front of line"| RG[Rate gate]:::svc
    RG -->|"admit at 10K per s"| TK[Signed token mint]:::svc
    TK -->|"token"| U
    U -.->|"poll position cached"| RS[Read service]:::svc

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Instead of randomly dropping buyers, I hold them in a real line — a Redis sorted set ordered by arrival — and admit the front at a tuned rate with signed tokens. That's a 300x reduction from three million to ten thousand a second, and it's fair-ish: you join once and keep your place rather than retry-storming. The buy endpoint only trusts the token, so the queue can't be skipped."

> 🧠 **If they ask "is this strict FIFO?":** "No — it's rough FIFO by arrival timestamp, which is what the business actually wants. Strict global FIFO across a million clients needs a global sequencer, a coordinator with its own ceiling and partition risk; the cost isn't worth it versus throughput and no-oversell."

### How do we handle the reservation timeout & release without re-overselling?

**#### Good: Redis TTL auto-expires the hold**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Let the hold tag vanish on its own after two minutes."
- **Approach:** set a 120s TTL on the reservation key; when it expires the hold is gone.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** TTL frees the *hold* but does **not** put the unit back in the counter — so expired holds silently shrink available stock; you under-sell (sell fewer than K). And if you naively re-increment on every expiry event you risk double-incrementing.
- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** the unit must return to the count *exactly once*.

**#### Great: Durable holds + idempotent release worker**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** TTL alone didn't re-increment the counter, so stock leaked. We add a worker that returns the unit exactly once.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A background clerk checks for expired holds and, for each one, puts exactly one shoe back on the shelf — and never twice."
- **Approach:** reservations are durable in Postgres with `expiresAt`. A release worker scans for `status=HELD AND expiresAt<now`, and in one transaction flips status to `EXPIRED` and re-increments the Redis counter. The status flip is the idempotency guard — if the row is already `EXPIRED`, the re-increment is skipped, so a worker re-run or two workers can't double-return.
- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** at most 1,000 holds, each ≤120s; the worker load is trivial (a few scans/s), so this never becomes a bottleneck.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| User pays at 119s | Checkout flips status to CONFIRMED first; worker sees non-HELD, skips release |
| Worker crashes mid-release | Transaction is atomic; on restart it re-scans, status guard prevents double-return |
| Two workers run concurrently | Row-level guard (status flip) makes one a no-op |
| Pay and release race at exactly TTL | Single transaction with status check resolves the winner; never both |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RES[(Reservation store Postgres)]:::store
    RW[Release worker]:::svc
    RES -->|"scan HELD and expired"| RW
    RW -->|"flip to EXPIRED in txn"| RES
    RW -->|"re-increment once"| IC[(Inventory counter Redis)]:::store
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Holds live durably in Postgres with an expiry time. A release worker finds expired-but-unpaid holds and, in one transaction, flips the status to expired and puts the unit back in the counter. The status flip is the idempotency guard — if a hold is already expired or already paid, the worker does nothing, so a unit can never be returned twice and we never re-oversell. The pay-versus-expire race is resolved by that same single transaction."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what about the pay-just-as-it-expires race?":** "Both checkout and the worker do a conditional update guarded by status — checkout only confirms a HELD reservation, the worker only releases a HELD one. The DB serializes them, so exactly one wins; the loser is a clean no-op."

### How does the buyer reliably see whether they won (read-your-own-writes)?

**#### Great: Read the reservation from the primary, status from cache**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Right after you tap buy, you read your own result from the authoritative copy, not a lagging cache."
- **Approach:** the coarse public status is eventual (CDN, ~1s). But the buyer's *own* reservation is read from the Postgres primary right after the write, so they immediately and correctly see HELD or SOLD_OUT. The reservation id is also returned synchronously in the 201, so the client often needs no read at all.
- ✅ **Why it works:** the one place strict read-your-own-writes matters (did I win?) reads the strongly-consistent store; the place where staleness is fine (public availability) reads the cache.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Public availability is allowed to be a second stale — it's just display. But the one thing a buyer must see correctly is their own result, so the reserve call returns the reservation synchronously and any follow-up read goes to the Postgres primary. Read-your-own-writes is guaranteed exactly where it matters and relaxed everywhere it doesn't."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the nines mean)**

- **Read/page path: ~99.99% (four nines = ~52 min/year down).** Mechanism: CDN edge across regions + stateless read service behind **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centres in one region; if one building loses power, another takes over automatically)*. Even if origin dies, the edge serves last-known status.
- **Buy/money path: ~99.9% (three nines = ~8.7 hr/year), with graceful shed above peak.** Mechanism: the waiting room *is* the availability mechanism — excess load gets a polite "you're in line" page, never a 500. Redis with a replica + AOF; Postgres multi-AZ with sync replica for orders.
- **Human downtime framing:** at peak the worst acceptable outcome is "you're in line," not "error" — the system stays *useful* even when saturated.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks | How it degrades (what USER sees) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| CDN edge | Page reads hit origin | Slightly slower page; status maybe 1s staler | CDN auto-reroutes to healthy edges |
| Redis inventory counter | Decrements stop | Buys briefly pause; user sees "try again in a moment" (no oversell) | Promote replica / replay AOF to exact count |
| Admission controller | No new admissions | Users wait longer in line (no error) | Stateless, autoscale; queue persists in Redis |
| Reservation store (PG) | Holds can't persist | Reserve returns retryable error; counter re-incremented (compensate) | Multi-AZ failover to sync replica |
| Payment processor | Charges fail | "Payment unavailable, your hold is kept for 2 min" | Retry with idempotency key; release on TTL if still down |
| Release worker | Expired stock not recycled | Slight under-sell (fewer than K sold) | Worker restarts, catches up; idempotent |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**

> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can afford to lose if it crashes right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is durably replicated before we acknowledge. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back after a crash.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Orders / payments | ≈0 | < 1 min | Postgres sync replica, multi-AZ failover; idempotency keys allow safe replay |
| Reservations | seconds | < 1 min | PG durable + Redis AOF; reservations re-derivable to rebuild counter |
| Inventory counter | seconds | < 30 s | Redis AOF + replica; rebuildable from reservations + orders (K − held − sold) |
| Coarse status cache | minutes (rebuildable) | instant | Recompute from counter; pure display |

**9D — Cost (rough monthly / per-sale)**

- **CDN egress — the #1 driver.** Serving a ~500KB page x several loads to 1M users is ~2,400 GB per sale event (~$120 in egress per big sale; trivial per-event but it's the dominant *bandwidth* line). Why: every byte to a browser is metered.
- **Burst compute (autoscaled app + admission tier) — #2.** The spike needs a lot of stateless capacity for minutes, ~$3K–8K per large sale event.
- **Always-on Redis + Postgres — #3.** Modest, ~$2K–5K/mo baseline.
- **Biggest optimization:** make the page fully static and edge-cached (one immutable object) and serve status as a tiny coarse JSON — this collapses both CDN egress weight and origin compute, since the expensive part is bytes-to-browser, not the DB.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When things fail, the user almost never sees an error — they see 'you're in line' or 'try again in a moment,' because the waiting room doubles as our load shield and we never oversell. Orders are RPO-zero on a synchronous Postgres replica; the inventory counter is rebuildable from reservations and orders, so even a total Redis loss recovers to the exact count. Cost-wise the surprise is it's CDN egress and burst compute that dominate, not the database — the DB only ever sees a thousand rows."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Redis atomic counter vs Postgres row lock for inventory**
1. **Chose & why:** Redis single-key atomic decrement — sub-ms, no disk lock, the cleanest no-oversell primitive at spike speed.
2. **Gave up:** in-memory volatility; we need an AOF mirror + durable reservations for recovery.
3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Like counting on a whiteboard everyone can see at once instead of passing one paper notebook around."
4. **Reverses when:** if the flash-sale spike is removed and traffic is gentle, a single Postgres row with a lock is simpler and needs no reconciliation.
5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "Inventory's in Redis for the atomic decrement; if we ever dropped the spike requirement I'd happily go back to one Postgres row."

**🔀 Decision: Virtual waiting room vs raw rate limiting**
1. **Chose & why:** an ordered queue that holds users — fair-ish and no retry storm — over a limiter that drops randomly.
2. **Gave up:** more moving parts (queue store, token minting, position polling).
3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A numbered deli line beats a bouncer who randomly shoves people out."
4. **Reverses when:** if peak admitted demand stays under ~80K/s, a simple limiter (or no throttle) suffices — drop the room.
5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "I built a real waiting room because random dropping is unfair and triggers retry storms; under ~80K/s I'd just rate-limit."

**🔀 Decision: Reserve-then-pay saga vs charge-inside-the-lock**
1. **Chose & why:** reserve atomically (ms), pay outside the lock (~800 ms external) — keeps lock-hold near zero so throughput stays high.
2. **Gave up:** a reservation can expire before payment; we need a release worker and reconciliation.
3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Set the shoe aside first, ring up the card after — don't make the next person wait at the register while you find your wallet."
4. **Reverses when:** if we owned payments in-house with sub-ms confirmation, a tighter single transaction becomes feasible.
5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "Payment never sits inside the lock — I reserve in a millisecond and pay after, with a timeout-release as the compensation."

**🔀 Decision: Rough FIFO vs strict global ordering**
1. **Chose & why:** rough FIFO by arrival timestamp per shard — cheap and good enough for the business.
2. **Gave up:** perfectly provable global ordering.
3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Roughly first-come-first-served, not a stopwatch-precise photo finish."
4. **Reverses when:** if a regulator or business demands provable strict ordering, add a global sequencer (and accept its ceiling).
5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "Fairness is rough FIFO — strict global ordering needs a global sequencer that becomes its own bottleneck, and the business doesn't need that precision."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd defend: Redis over a Postgres lock for the atomic decrement — I'd flip back if the spike went away. A real waiting room over random rate limiting — I'd drop it under ~80K/s. Reserve-then-pay over charging in the lock — I'd tighten it only if we owned payments. And rough FIFO over strict ordering — I'd add a sequencer only if someone proved we needed provable fairness. Every one traces back to protecting that single counter while keeping the site up."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**Core algorithm / hardest part**

**❓ "Two buyers race for the last unit — who wins, and how do you guarantee it?"**
The mechanism: the decrement is a single atomic Lua step in Redis — `if count>0 then count−1; return count else −1` — so Redis serializes both writers; one sees `0` (won), the other `−1` (sold-out). There's no read-then-write window for them to interleave in.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "The store does 'subtract one if any left' as one uninterruptible action, so only one of them can be the one who took the last shoe."
💬 "It's one atomic decrement — two racers physically can't both come back non-negative."

**❓ "Why not just use a database transaction with a row lock?"**
The mechanism: SELECT FOR UPDATE locks the inventory row on disk and holds it until commit; under a ~3M/s spike the connection pool (a fixed ~200 connections) exhausts in under a second and the DB stalls for everyone. Redis's in-memory single-key op serializes without a client-held lock.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "A DB lock makes everyone queue at one notebook; under a million people that notebook never gets passed around fast enough and the whole DB freezes."
💬 "Row locks are correct but they exhaust the connection pool in seconds at this scale."

**❓ "How do you decide the admission rate?"**
The mechanism: set it well below the single-key ceiling (~100K/s) — we use ~10K/s, a 300x cut from the 3M/s spike. At 10K/s the 1,000 units sell out in ~100 ms, so the rate just needs to be safely under the ceiling, not high.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We only let through as many as the counter can comfortably handle — way fewer than show up."
💬 "Admit at ten thousand a second — comfortably under the hundred-thousand ceiling."

**Failure scenarios**

**❓ "What if Redis (the counter) crashes mid-sale?"**
The mechanism: Redis runs with an append-only file and a replica; on crash we promote the replica or replay the AOF to the exact remaining. As a backstop, the true count is rebuildable from Postgres: K minus held minus sold.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We keep a written log of every decrement and a hot standby, and we can always recompute the count from the orders and holds we've durably saved."
💬 "Replay the AOF or promote the replica — and worst case rebuild from reservations and orders."

**❓ "What if the payment processor goes down?"**
The mechanism: payment is outside the lock, so a processor outage doesn't touch inventory. The user sees "payment unavailable, your hold is kept for two minutes"; checkout retries with the idempotency key. If still down at TTL, the release worker returns the unit.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Your shoe stays held while we retry the card; if the card service is still down when your two minutes run out, the shoe goes back for someone else."
💬 "Inventory and payment are decoupled — a processor outage just keeps your hold and retries idempotently."

**❓ "What if the release worker double-runs and returns a unit twice?"**
The mechanism: release is guarded by a status flip in one Postgres transaction — it only acts on `HELD` rows and flips them to `EXPIRED`. A second run finds the row already EXPIRED and does nothing, so the counter can't be double-incremented.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "The clerk marks the tag 'returned' as it puts the shoe back, so it can't accidentally put the same shoe back twice."
💬 "The status flip is the idempotency guard — double-runs are no-ops."

**Scale / hot-spot**

**❓ "The inventory key is a single hot key — isn't that the bottleneck?"**
The mechanism: yes, deliberately — it's the one global coordinator. We relieve it by admission control (10K/s ≪ 100K/s ceiling) rather than by sharding, because for K=1,000 the problem is write rate, not the count. For very large K we'd shard into stripes of K/N with a reconciler.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "It's one hot number on purpose; we keep the crowd thin in front of it instead of splitting it, unless the inventory itself is huge."
💬 "Admission control is the relief; sharding only earns its keep when K is large."

**❓ "How do you handle a million people polling the status page?"**
The mechanism: the page and status are served from the CDN edge; a coarse available/sold-out caches ~1s, so 500K reads/s collapse to ~500/s at origin (99.9% hit). The origin never feels the read storm.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "A worldwide cache near users answers almost all the refreshes; our servers see only the handful that miss."
💬 "Half a million reads a second become a few hundred after the CDN."

**Consistency / correctness**

**❓ "Can the same order be placed twice?"**
The mechanism: checkout carries an idempotency key; the first call creates the order, retries with the same key return that same order rather than creating a second. The reservation→order transition is a single Postgres transaction.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "If your phone retries, we recognize it's the same attempt and just show you the order you already have."
💬 "Idempotency key collapses retries to exactly one order and one charge."

**❓ "Can you ever oversell?"**
The mechanism: no — the only mutation of inventory is the atomic decrement, bounded so exactly K writers come back non-negative; releases are idempotent re-increments guarded by status; and the count is reconcilable from durable holds and orders.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "There's exactly one place the count goes down, and it physically can't go below zero, so we can't sell a unit that doesn't exist."
💬 "One atomic decrement, idempotent release — the invariant holds ≤ K is enforced by construction."

**❓ "Does a buyer reliably see whether they won?"**
The mechanism: the reserve call returns HELD/SOLD_OUT synchronously, and any follow-up reads the reservation from the Postgres primary (read-your-own-writes). Only the *public* coarse status is eventual.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "You instantly see your own result from the real record; only the general 'is it sold out' number is allowed to lag a second."
💬 "Strong read-your-own-writes where it matters, eventual only on public display."

**Security**

**❓ "How do you stop someone tampering with the price?"**
The mechanism: the client never sends a price; the server reads `amount` from the Sale config at checkout. Identity is from the JWT, not a body field, and reservations bind to that identity.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "The price comes from our database, never the request, so editing the request can't change what you pay."
💬 "Price and identity are server-side — the client can't forge either."

**❓ "What stops a bot from skipping the waiting room?"**
The mechanism: the buy endpoint only accepts a short-lived, server-signed admission token; without a valid token you get a 401. Joins are dedup'd per user and rate-limited per IP.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "You can't reach the buy button without a signed pass we handed out in line, and we hand out one per person."
💬 "Signed admission tokens make the queue un-skippable."

**❓ "How do you defend against scraping the live count?"**
The mechanism: the public status is coarse (available/sold-out), never the exact number, and it's served from cache; the precise remaining lives only in Redis behind the buy path.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We never tell the public the exact number left, so there's nothing precise to scrape."
💬 "Coarse status only — the real count never leaves the buy path."

**Cost**

**❓ "What's the most expensive part?"**
The mechanism: CDN egress (bytes served to a million browsers) and burst compute for the spike — not the database, which only ever holds ~1,000 rows.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "The big bill is sending the page to a million phones and the short burst of servers, not the tiny database."
💬 "Egress and burst compute dominate; the DB is a rounding error."

**❓ "How would you cut cost the most?"**
The mechanism: make the sale page a single immutable, fully edge-cached object and serve status as a tiny coarse JSON — this minimizes egress weight and origin compute, the two dominant drivers.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Make the page one static thing the cache can serve forever, so we ship fewer bytes from our servers."
💬 "Static, immutable page + coarse status JSON — that's where the savings are."

**Extensibility**

**❓ "How would you support multiple SKUs / many simultaneous sales?"**
The mechanism: each sale is a separate counter key and queue keyed by `saleId`; sales are independent, so we just provision more Redis shards and the admission tier scales horizontally. No global coordination across sales.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Each drop is its own self-contained line and counter, so adding more drops is just adding more of the same."
💬 "Per-sale keys mean sales never contend with each other — scale out, don't redesign."

**❓ "How would you add per-user quantity (buy up to N)?"**
The mechanism: change the decrement to subtract N (still atomic, with a floor at zero) and enforce the per-user cap with a dedup key combining userId+saleId; the reservation carries quantity.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We'd let the counter drop by however many you buy, in one atomic step, and still cap how many one person can take."
💬 "Decrement-by-N stays atomic; the per-user cap is a dedup key."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A flash-sale system sells exactly K limited items to a huge crowd the instant the doors open, without ever overselling. The design splits into two halves. The read half is pure CDN — a million people polling a coarse available-or-sold-out page, of which the edge absorbs 99.9% so the origin sees only a few hundred a second. The write half is the hard part: a virtual waiting room sheds a three-million-a-second stampede down to a safe ten thousand a second with signed admission tokens, and behind it sits a single atomic Redis decrement — the one global coordinator — that guarantees exactly K winners because two racers can never both come back non-negative. The two halves connect through reservations: a winner gets a two-minute hold, pays an external processor outside any lock with an idempotency key, and if they don't pay, a release worker returns the unit to the counter exactly once. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: protect the single contended inventory counter, and keep the crowd away from it."
